title: Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years
subtitle: Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"
caption:
Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years, 2021
Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"

This stacked bar chart displays the distribution of public opinion across eleven countries regarding whether artificial intelligence will mostly help or mostly harm people over the next two decades, based on a 2021 survey of roughly 1,000 nationally representative adults per country. The horizontal bars are segmented into five categories: "Mostly help" (blue), "Neither" (green), "No response" (purple), "No opinion or don't know" (tan), and "Mostly harm" (maroon), with each bar summing to 100%. In Japan and China, the largest share of respondents—61% in both—expect AI to mostly help, with only 13% in each anticipating mostly harm. Germany follows closely with 60% expecting mostly help and 18% expecting mostly harm. Italy shows a split: half expect mostly help, while 32% expect mostly harm. The United Kingdom and Canada show more balanced distributions, with 47% and 40% respectively expecting mostly help, and 29% in each expecting mostly harm. France and the United States each have 40% and 35% expecting mostly help, matched by 36% and 35% expecting mostly harm. India’s responses mirror the U.S. pattern: 33% expect mostly help, 36% expect mostly harm. Indonesia stands out with 44% selecting “no opinion or don’t know,” the highest such share among all countries shown, while only 22% expect mostly help and 33% expect mostly harm. Pakistan has the lowest share expecting mostly help at 19%, and the highest share expecting mostly harm at 52%. Across the full set of countries, the proportion expecting AI to mostly help ranges from 19% to 61%, while the proportion expecting mostly harm ranges from 13% to 52%. The dominant visible pattern is that expectations of AI’s impact vary substantially by country, with no single global consensus emerging from the data.
